text:
This article considers the present plight of Youth Ministry in light of the doctrine of *Sola Scriptura* in Reformed theology. Despite an abundance of strategic initiatives, modern Youth Ministry does not typically result in young people being equipped with enduring levels of spiritual maturity. One of the major contributing factors in this regard is the isolation of adolescents from the mainstream of ordinary adult life in the world around them—especially their parents—and from an interconnected and fully functioning body of believers across the various generations within the life of a local church.

From a biblical perspective this study explores the possibility of Youth Ministry shifting towards a family focused paradigm. Parents remain the primary spiritual teachers of their children; but the church functions as a key support to the family, equipping them to become their children’s primary spiritual educators. The study identifies the roles of parents, youth workers and the church and brings a renewed focus to equipping them to work together in a holistic discipleship model, which moves beyond the segregation of isolated youth-focused programs.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
